content.text: A deprecation announcement without a grace period is a removal notice. The grace period is the window between when a version is declared deprecated and when it is actually removed — the time callers have to migrate safely. Getting the grace period right means balancing the maintenance cost of running deprecated versions against the migration risk for callers that need time to move.

A grace period is the minimum time that must elapse between a deprecation announcement and a hard sunset. It is the contractual window during which the deprecated prompt template or model version continues to function — potentially at a reduced support level — so that callers have time to migrate to the successor version.

Grace periods exist because migration is not instantaneous. Callers need time to update code, run evaluations, conduct canary deployments, and get sign-off for production changes. The grace period policy defines how much time they get, and under what conditions that time can be extended.
callers with production SLAs cannot accept a prompt version that stops working without warning. A grace period makes the sunset predictable.
migrations carry regression risk. Teams need time to run evals, deploy canaries, and validate in production before cutting over fully.

teams that have been burned by short-notice removals stop pinning to specific versions — they use floating aliases instead, which introduces silent quality changes. Generous grace periods build the trust that encourages responsible version pinning.
enterprise contracts often require minimum notice periods before material changes. The grace period policy must accommodate these contractual obligations.
Reduces production incidents caused by unexpected version removals.
Gives teams time to include migrations in their normal sprint planning rather than emergency-patching.
Allows quality evaluation of the successor before full commitment, reducing regression risk.
Creates a formal record of when deprecation was announced, which can be used in SLA dispute resolution.
Longer grace periods increase the cost of maintaining deprecated versions. Running two model versions in parallel doubles the model serving infrastructure cost.

Extended grace periods mean longer windows during which callers delay migration. Some callers will not migrate until the day before the hard sunset — making the maintenance window longer than necessary.
Emergency removals (security vulnerabilities) may not be able to honor the standard grace period. The policy must explicitly carve out this exception.

Think of a grace period as a lease on the deprecated version. The tenant (caller) has until the lease end date (hard sunset) to vacate. The landlord (platform team) cannot evict before the lease end, but can choose not to fix non-critical issues in the leased space after the notice is served. Both parties know the terms upfront and can plan accordingly.
Grace period extensions are like lease renewals — they require justification, come at additional cost, and must be requested before the original lease expires.

attrs.alt: Grace period policy: factors determining grace period length, coexistence strategies (header-based, param-based, traffic weight routing), support level degradation, and sunset ceremony checklist

content.text: Factors That Determine Grace Period Length
The minimum grace period is the maximum of the values driven by each factor:
Coexistence Strategies During the Grace Period
During the grace period, old and new versions must coexist. Three routing strategies enable this:
; callers still on the old version send
(or omit the header to get the default, which is the old version during the grace period, switching to the new version at hard sunset).
For REST APIs where adding request headers is difficult, use a query parameter:
remains valid. After the hard sunset, it returns 410.

callers are blocked from registering with v2 (new caller onboarding requires v3). Existing callers retain their v2 routing until they explicitly migrate or the grace period ends. Traffic metrics show the v2 share declining as teams migrate.
Support Levels During the Grace Period
Support does not remain constant throughout the grace period. It degrades in phases to incentivize timely migration:
all bug fixes applied to the deprecated version. Security patches deployed. On-call response within SLA. Callers experience no degradation relative to the pre-deprecation state.
security patches only. No new feature work or non-critical bug fixes. On-call response SLA relaxed to 2×. This phase is communicated explicitly in the deprecation notification.
version removed. All requests return 410 Gone. No support provided. Callers must be on the successor version.
Some callers have legitimate reasons to request an extension — a blocker that prevents migration within the standard window. Extensions are not automatic and must be explicitly approved:
legal or compliance review blocking migration, an enterprise SLA obligation that requires longer notice, a data migration of unusual complexity (e.g., re-indexing a 100M-document corpus).
team is too busy, migration was not started early enough, preference to stay on the old version. Workload and prioritization decisions are not grounds for extension.

extension request must be filed as a GitHub issue comment on the deprecation tracking issue. Requires sign-off from VP Engineering and platform team lead. Includes a cost-impact assessment of the maintenance extension.
extensions are granted in 30-day increments, not open-ended. Each extension must include a migration completion date.

## Extension Request Template

**Issue:** #789 (prompt-summarization-v2 sunset)
**Requested by:** payments-team
**Requested extension:** 30 days (new sunset: 2026-11-01)

**Reason:** Data migration of 50M conversation records to new memory schema
blocks output parser migration. Migration requires 6-week DBA engagement
already scheduled for Q4.

**Evidence:** JIRA ticket ENG-4521 (DBA engagement)
Legal compliance review (attached)

**Migration plan during extension:**
- Week 1-2: DBA completes schema migration
- Week 3-4: Parser update and unit tests
- Week 5: Canary at 5% for 7 days
- Week 6: Full cutover

**Cost acknowledgment:** Extension costs estimated at $3,200/month in
duplicated model serving infrastructure. Payments team accepts this cost.

**Approval:** @vp-engineering @platform-lead
Cost Implications of Maintaining Deprecated Versions
Running deprecated versions in parallel with the successor has direct and indirect costs:
model serving infrastructure must be provisioned for both versions. If the deprecated version has 20% of traffic and the successor 80%, the old version still requires dedicated serving capacity.

the platform team spends approximately 5% of an engineer's time per deprecated version on security patches, monitoring, and caller queries during the grace period. Three concurrent deprecations consume 15% of an engineer.
engineering time spent maintaining deprecated versions is time not spent on new features or the next architectural improvement.

track the cost of each deprecated version in a deprecation registry. This data makes the extension request approval decision concrete: approving a 30-day extension for a version costing $3,000/month is a $3,000 decision.
Sunset Ceremony: The Final Traffic Cutover Procedure
The sunset ceremony is the planned event at the hard sunset date that decommissions the deprecated version. It is not an emergency — it is a ceremony, planned in advance, with a defined runbook:
check access logs for any remaining traffic to the deprecated version. If non-zero, identify the caller and escalate. Do not proceed to sunset with active callers.
set routing weight to 0% for the deprecated version. Wait 15 minutes. Verify no traffic in metrics.
update the API router to return 410 Gone (with Link header to successor) for any requests that specify the deprecated version, rather than silently routing to the successor.
move the prompt template to a read-only archive storage bucket. Retain for at least 2 years for audit and forensic purposes. Tag the model version record as archived.
shut down serving instances for the deprecated model version. Reclaim the infrastructure.
close the GitHub tracking issue with a sunset summary: caller count migrated, total migration time, infrastructure cost savings achieved.
a brief write-up of what went well (callers migrated ahead of schedule), what went poorly (callers needed extensions), and what will be done differently next time.
How to Implement the Grace Period Policy

# deprecation-registry.yaml — single source of truth for all active deprecations
deprecations:
  - id: prompt-summarization-v2
    type: prompt_template
    announced: 2026-07-18
    grace_period_days: 90
    hard_sunset: 2026-10-01
    support_level:
      full_sla_until: 2026-09-17    # Day 60
      best_effort_from: 2026-09-17
    callers:
      - team: summarisation-service
        migrated: false
        contact: eng-lead@summarisation-team.com
      - team: content-pipeline
        migrated: false
        contact: eng-lead@content-team.com
    successor: prompt-summarization-v3
    cost_per_month_usd: 2400
    extensions: []
    owner: platform-team
    tracking_issue: https://github.com/company/ai-platform/issues/789

A GitHub Actions workflow reads this registry daily and sends Slack reminders at Day 30, Day 60, and Day 80 milestones. At the hard sunset date, it opens a sunset ceremony checklist issue automatically.
An AI platform team deprecates a customer support model pinned to
(deprecated by Anthropic). The grace period analysis:
Caller criticality: two production-critical callers → 90-day minimum.
Migration complexity: output format changed in successor model → parser update required → 60-day minimum.
SLA obligations: one enterprise customer contract requires 90-day notice → 90-day minimum.

The 90-day grace period is announced on Day 0. Both production callers complete migration by Day 65. The enterprise customer confirms at Day 70. Sunset ceremony runs on Day 90 with zero active callers. Infrastructure cost savings of $4,800 (2 months × $2,400/month) are achieved.
Setting grace periods by habit rather than analysis:
defaulting to 30 days for every deprecation regardless of caller complexity or SLA obligations. Run the factor analysis for each deprecation.
Running the sunset ceremony without verifying zero traffic:
removing a version that still has active callers causes immediate production incidents. Always check traffic in the 7 days before sunset.
extensions should be treated as budget decisions. The cost of 30 more days of deprecated version maintenance must be explicitly acknowledged.
deleting deprecated prompt templates entirely prevents forensic analysis of historical AI outputs. Always archive to read-only storage.
Run grace period factor analysis before setting the sunset date. The hardest-to-migrate caller determines the minimum grace period.
Track migration progress weekly. Traffic metrics showing v2 share declining toward zero are the only reliable indicator that all callers are migrating.
Communicate support level changes explicitly at announcement time. Callers should know from Day 0 that support drops to best-effort at Day 60.
Run a sunset ceremony checklist item to verify zero traffic in the 7 days before the hard sunset. Block the ceremony if any traffic remains.
Publish the cost of each active deprecation monthly. Visibility into ongoing maintenance costs motivates timely migration and makes extension requests take budget seriously.
YAML file in the platform repository, read by GitHub Actions to automate reminders and ceremony checklists.
with Grafana dashboard showing migration progress per caller.
GitHub Actions with scheduled workflow that reads the deprecation registry and sends Slack messages at configured milestone days.
Deprecated versions that contain known security vulnerabilities must follow the emergency 7-day grace period rather than the standard policy. Security risk outweighs caller convenience.

Archived prompt templates must be stored with the same access controls as production data — they may contain PII from the queries that shaped them. Use role-based access control for the archive bucket.
The 410 Gone response after sunset should include a
header set to a far-future date to prevent retry storms from callers that have not yet updated their error handling.

During the grace period, monitor for any anomalous access to the deprecated version — sudden spikes may indicate a new caller that was not in the original caller list and has not received the deprecation notice.

A grace period is a contractual window between deprecation announcement and hard removal. Its length is determined by the hardest factor: caller criticality, migration complexity, and SLA obligations.
The minimum grace period is the maximum of all factor scores. Production-critical callers or enterprise SLAs typically drive this to 90-120 days.
Support degrades in phases: full SLA until Day 60, best-effort until Day 90, nothing after the hard sunset. Communicate this at announcement time.
Coexistence during the grace period uses header-based routing, query parameter routing, or traffic weight routing — all of which allow old and new versions to run in parallel.
Extensions must be justified (compliance blocker, SLA obligation, unusual migration complexity) and cost-assessed. They are not granted for workload reasons.
Track the cost of each deprecated version monthly. Maintenance cost is a forcing function for timely migration.
The sunset ceremony verifies zero traffic in the 7 days before cutover, flips routing, returns 410 Gone, archives the prompt, and publishes a retrospective.
Never sunset a version with non-zero traffic. Verify active caller count is zero before the ceremony.
